highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.166: Fallback Models & Glob Deny Rules
highlights.summary: This release adds a `fallbackModel` setting that lets you configure up to three backup models to try automatically when your primary model is overloaded or unavailable, reducing disruptions during heavy usage. It also introduces glob pattern support in deny rules, so you can use `"*"` to block all tools at once for tighter permission control. If you're using `--fallback-model` in scripts, note it now also works in interactive sessions.

highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.163: Version Enforcement & Plugin Management
highlights.summary: Admins and teams can now use `requiredMinimumVersion` and `requiredMaximumVersion` managed settings to ensure all users run an approved version of Claude Code, preventing startup if the version is out of range. A new `/plugin list` command with `--enabled`/`--disabled` flags makes it easier to audit and manage installed plugins directly from the terminal. These features are especially useful for organizations standardizing Claude Code deployments.

highlights.title: Introducing Claude Opus 4.8
highlights.summary: Anthropic has released Claude Opus 4.8, the latest addition to the Opus model family, bringing improved capabilities to Claude Code users who rely on the most powerful Anthropic models. If you use Claude Code with the Opus tier, you may want to update your model configuration to take advantage of the newest release. Check the Anthropic blog for full details on what's new and improved.

highlights.title: Learn 80% of Claude Code in 10 Minutes (2026 Tutorial)
highlights.summary: This concise YouTube tutorial is designed to get you productive with Claude Code quickly by covering the most commonly used features in just 10 minutes. It's a great starting point if you feel overwhelmed by the full documentation and want a fast, practical overview. After watching, you'll have enough knowledge to start using Claude Code for real coding tasks in VS Code.

highlights.title: Show HN: Lessons Learned from Running Claude Code Swarms at Scale
highlights.summary: A developer shares hard-won lessons from building and scaling a "fleet" system that orchestrates multiple Claude Code agents in parallel using a Python orchestrator. The post covers practical challenges like coordination, cost management, and reliability when running many agents simultaneously. If you're curious about multi-agent Claude Code workflows, this is a valuable real-world case study.

highlights.title: Claude Code and Codex Can Have Real-Time Conversation via Git
highlights.summary: This article explores an unconventional technique where Claude Code and OpenAI Codex can communicate and collaborate in real time by exchanging messages through a shared Git repository. It's a creative workaround for orchestrating multiple AI coding agents without a custom API integration. Developers interested in cross-agent workflows or hybrid AI setups will find this experiment thought-provoking.

highlights.title: Headroom: Optimize Claude Code Prompts to Double Your Usage
highlights.summary: Headroom is a macOS menu bar app that silently trims prompt bloat, strips boilerplate, and compresses documents before they reach Claude Code, helping you get roughly 2x more usage out of your existing Claude subscription. It works passively in the background without changing your workflow, making it a low-friction way to stretch your token budget. This is especially useful for developers who regularly hit usage limits on the standard Claude plan.

tips_of_the_day: Configure a `fallbackModel` in your Claude Code settings with 2-3 backup models (e.g., a smaller or different Anthropic model) so your coding session continues uninterrupted even when your primary model hits capacity during peak hours.

Before pasting large files or documentation into Claude Code, manually trim irrelevant sections like license headers, verbose comments, or auto-generated boilerplate — smaller, focused context leads to faster and more accurate responses.

If your team uses Claude Code, use the `requiredMinimumVersion` managed setting to lock everyone to a tested version, preventing unexpected behavior caused by one teammate running a much older or newer release than the rest of the group.
